lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
The Saracens, under Mavia their Queen, embrace Christianity; and Moses, a Pious Monk, is consecrated their Bishop.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: The Saracens, under Mavia their Queen, embrace Christianity; and Moses, a Pious Monk, is consecrated their Bishop.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: No sooner had the emperor departed from Antioch, than the Saracens, who had before been in alliance with the Romans, revolted from them, being led by Mavia their queen, whose husband was then dead.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: All the regions of the East therefore were at that time ravaged by the Saracens: but a certain divine Providence repressed their fury in the manner I am about to describe.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: A person named Moses, a Saracen by birth, who led a monastic life in the desert, became exceedingly eminent for his piety, faith, and miracles.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Mavia the queen of the Saracens was therefore desirous that this person should be constituted bishop over her nation, and promised on the condition to terminate the war.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Roman generals considering that a peace founded on such terms would be extremely advantageous, gave immediate directions for its ratification.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Moses was accordingly seized, and brought from the desert to Alexandria, in order that he might there be invested with the bishopric: but on his presentation for that purpose to Lucius, who at that time presided over the churches in that city, he refused to be ordained by him, protesting against it in these words: ‘I account myself indeed unworthy of the sacred office; but if the exigencies of the state require my bearing it, it shall not be by Lucius laying his hand on me, for it has been filled with blood.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When Lucius told him that it was his duty to learn from him the principles of religion, and not to utter reproachful language, Moses replied, ‘Matters of faith are not now in question: but your infamous practices against the brethren sufficiently prove that your doctrines are not Christian.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For a Christian is “no striker, reviles not, does not fight”; for “it becomes not a servant of the Lord to fight.”

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But your deeds cry out against you by those who have been sent into exile, who have been exposed to the wild beasts, and who had been delivered up to the flames.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those things which our own eyes have beheld are far more convincing than what we receive from the report of another.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As Moses expressed these and other similar sentiments his friends took him to the mountains, that he might receive ordination from those bishops who lived in exile there.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Moses having thus been consecrated, the Saracen war was terminated; and so scrupulously did Mavia observe the peace thus entered into with the Romans that she gave her daughter in marriage to Victor the commander-in-chief of the Roman army.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such were the transactions in relation to the Saracens.
